title: M-Pesa fees demystified
text: M-Pesa is so woven into daily life in Kenya that it's easy to stop noticing its fees entirely — they're just small numbers that disappear from a balance. But over a month, those small numbers add up to a real expense, and understanding the *shape* of how they work can help you route money more cheaply without much extra effort.

title: M-Pesa fees demystified
heading: The core idea: tiered pricing
text:
Almost every M-Pesa transaction type uses **tiered pricing** — the fee depends on which "band" your amount falls into, not a flat percentage. Sending KES 100 and sending KES 99 might cost the same, because they're in the same tier, while sending KES 101 could jump to the next tier's fee. This means amounts that sit just above a tier boundary are often quietly more expensive *per shilling* than amounts just below it.

The exact tiers and fees change from time to time (Safaricom periodically revises them, and there have been periods of fee waivers for certain transaction types). Rather than memorizing numbers that may be outdated by the time you read this, the more durable habit is: **check the fee shown on the confirmation screen before you confirm** — it's almost always displayed, and a moment's pause here is free.

id: kenya-money/01-mpesa-fees-demystified#Why different transaction types cost differently
title: M-Pesa fees demystified
heading: Why different transaction types cost differently
text:
- **Send Money (person to person)** — generally has its own fee tier structure.
- **Withdraw (cash out at an agent)** — typically has a different, often higher, fee structure than sending — agents need to be compensated for providing physical cash.
- **Pay Bill** — used for paying businesses, utilities, and institutions with a business number. Fees here are often lower than Withdraw, and at various times certain Pay Bill transactions (like utility payments) have been fee-free or reduced.
- **Buy Goods (Till Number)** — used for in-person retail payments. Historically, Buy Goods transactions have often been free or very low-cost for the customer, which is part of why many small businesses prefer customers pay via Till rather than Send Money.
- **M-Pesa to Bank / Bank to M-Pesa** — has its own fee structure, generally separate from person-to-person transfers.

The practical takeaway: **the same amount of money, moved the same distance (person to person, roughly), can cost different amounts depending on which "rail" you use.** If a shop offers both a Till number and a personal phone number to pay, the Till option is often cheaper for you — worth a glance before paying.

id: kenya-money/01-mpesa-fees-demystified#A simple habit: route, don't just send
title: M-Pesa fees demystified
heading: A simple habit: route, don't just send
text:
Before moving money, a quick mental check — "is there a cheaper *type* of transaction for what I'm actually doing?" — can save real money over a year:

- Paying a shop? Buy Goods (Till) is often cheaper than Send Money to a personal number.
- Paying a bill (electricity, water, school fees)? Pay Bill, not Send Money to someone's personal number who then pays on your behalf.
- Moving your own money to savings? Many banks and SACCOs have direct M-Pesa integration that may cost less than withdraw-then-deposit.

title: M-Pesa fees demystified
heading: Fuliza — what it actually is
text:
Fuliza is M-Pesa's **overdraft facility** — if you try to send money or pay for something and your M-Pesa balance is insufficient, Fuliza can cover the shortfall, *if* you've opted in and are eligible. The transaction goes through, and the amount you borrowed (plus a fee) is deducted automatically the next time money lands in your M-Pesa account.

The convenience is real — a transaction that would otherwise fail goes through instead. But it's still a loan, with a cost attached, and because repayment happens automatically and immediately when money arrives, it's easy to lose track of how often it's being used. If a meaningful chunk of your incoming money is routinely going straight to clearing a Fuliza balance, that's worth treating as a budget line item (back to Module 1) rather than background noise.

title: M-Pesa fees demystified
heading: Ask Orchestra-Core
text: If you're not sure which transaction type is cheapest for something you do often, Ask Orchestra-Core can walk through the general structure of M-Pesa's fee tiers and how to think about choosing between options — while reminding you to confirm the actual fee shown on your screen, since rates do change over time.

title: SACCOs vs banks — which is better for you?
text: Both SACCOs and banks let you save and borrow money — but they're built on fundamentally different models, and that difference shows up in dividends, loan terms, and how easily you can get your money out.

title: SACCOs vs banks — which is better for you?
heading: The core difference: ownership
text:
A **bank** is a company. When you deposit money, you're a customer — the bank's profits go to its shareholders, who are generally not you (unless you happen to also own bank shares).

A **SACCO (Savings and Credit Co-operative Organisation)** is a co-operative, owned by its members. When you deposit (technically, your deposits are often structured partly as "shares"), you're a part-owner. Profits the SACCO makes are distributed back to members as **dividends**, in proportion to their savings/shares — so members are, in a sense, both customers and shareholders at once.

This single difference in ownership explains most of the practical differences below.

title: SACCOs vs banks — which is better for you?
heading: Returns on your savings
text:
Bank savings accounts typically offer modest interest rates on regular savings — often quite low in real terms once inflation is considered.

SACCOs often pay annual **dividends** on members' deposits that are meaningfully higher than typical bank savings interest — sometimes in the double digits, depending on the SACCO's performance that year. This is one of the most commonly cited reasons people join SACCOs: the same money, sitting in savings, tends to grow faster.

The tradeoff: bank interest is typically credited regularly (e.g., monthly), while SACCO dividends are usually declared and paid **once a year**, after the SACCO's annual general meeting (AGM) — so the "return" is real, but it arrives on a different schedule, and isn't guaranteed in advance the way a bank's advertised rate is (it depends on the SACCO's actual performance that year).

title: SACCOs vs banks — which is better for you?
heading: Borrowing: the multiplier effect
text:
This is where SACCOs often stand out most. Many SACCOs let members borrow a **multiple of their savings/deposits** — for example, a member with KES 100,000 saved might be eligible to borrow 3x or 4x that amount (the exact multiplier varies by SACCO and loan product), often at interest rates lower than typical bank personal loans, with repayment terms designed around salaried members' pay cycles.

Banks, by contrast, generally assess loan eligibility through credit scoring, income verification, and collateral — a process that can be more flexible in *what* it considers, but is often less generous in *how much* relative to your existing savings, especially for members without a long credit history.

title: SACCOs vs banks — which is better for you?
heading: Liquidity: getting your money out
text:
This is the area where banks generally have the edge. Bank accounts (beyond fixed deposits) are typically accessible on demand — withdraw anytime, often instantly via ATM, agent, or mobile banking.

SACCO deposits/shares are often less liquid — withdrawing your savings (as opposed to just the dividend) may involve a formal process, sometimes with notice periods, and withdrawing your full "share capital" might affect your loan eligibility or even your membership status, depending on the SACCO's rules.

title: SACCOs vs banks — which is better for you?
heading: Regulation: who's watching
text: Banks in Kenya are regulated by the **Central Bank of Kenya (CBK)**. SACCOs that take deposits are regulated by the **Sacco Societies Regulatory Authority (SASRA)**. Both are real regulatory regimes with licensing and oversight requirements — but they're different bodies with different rules, which is worth knowing if you ever need to raise a concern about either type of institution.

title: SACCOs vs banks — which is better for you?
heading: Two broad types of SACCOs
text:
- **Employer-based / workplace SACCOs** — membership tied to working at a particular organization or in a particular sector (common among teachers, civil servants, university staff, etc.). Contributions are often deducted directly from payroll.
- **Community-based SACCOs** — open membership, often organized around a geographic area, a common interest group, or a broader professional category, not tied to a single employer.

title: SACCOs vs banks — which is better for you?
heading: So which is better?
text:
For most people, the honest answer is **both, doing different jobs**:

- A **bank account** for day-to-day liquidity — the money you need access to without delay, and for services (cards, international transfers, business banking) that SACCOs typically don't provide.
- A **SACCO** for medium-to-long-term savings and as a borrowing tool — money you're disciplined about not touching often, that grows faster via dividends, and that gives you access to better loan terms when you need them.

The "emergency fund" lesson (Money Basics, Module 4) touches on exactly this kind of blending — different accounts for different jobs, chosen deliberately rather than by accident.

title: SACCOs vs banks — which is better for you?
heading: Ask Orchestra-Core
text: If you're trying to decide where to direct your savings, Ask Orchestra-Core can walk through the general tradeoffs between SACCO and bank savings for your situation — without recommending a specific SACCO or bank, since the right fit depends on details (employer affiliation, specific products, current rates) that only you and the institution itself can confirm.

title: Buying NSE shares step-by-step
text: > **Before we start:** this lesson explains the *mechanics* of how share-buying works on the Nairobi Securities Exchange (NSE) — the accounts, steps, and costs involved. It does not recommend any specific company, broker, or platform, and isn't a suggestion that buying shares is right for you. Whether and what to invest in depends on your own goals, timeline, and circumstances — and ideally, a solid emergency fund (Money Basics, Module 4) before any money goes toward shares at all.

id: kenya-money/03-buying-nse-shares#The big picture: what you're actually doing
title: Buying NSE shares step-by-step
heading: The big picture: what you're actually doing
text: Buying a share means buying a small ownership stake in a company listed on the NSE — companies across sectors like banking, telecommunications, manufacturing, and agriculture are listed there. Like most modern stock exchanges, shares are held electronically, not as physical paper certificates — which is where the CDS comes in.

title: Buying NSE shares step-by-step
heading: Step 1: Understand the CDS
text:
The **Central Depository System (CDS)**, operated by the Central Depository & Settlement Corporation (CDSC), is the electronic system that records who owns what shares. To buy NSE shares, you need a **CDS account** — sometimes called a CDS number — which is essentially your personal electronic "vault" where your shares are recorded as belonging to you.

You don't open a CDS account directly with CDSC yourself in the typical retail process — it's opened *for* you through a licensed intermediary, which brings us to step 2.

id: kenya-money/03-buying-nse-shares#Step 2: Choose a licensed stockbroker or investment bank
title: Buying NSE shares step-by-step
heading: Step 2: Choose a licensed stockbroker or investment bank
text:
To trade on the NSE, you go through a **licensed stockbroker or investment bank** — these are the firms authorized to execute trades on the exchange on your behalf. Several licensed firms in Kenya offer this service, including some with mobile apps that have made the process significantly more accessible than it used to be (historically, opening an account often meant visiting a physical office with paperwork).

When choosing, things worth comparing (without this lesson recommending any specific one) include: the account-opening process (online vs. in-person), minimum amounts if any, the trading platform's usability, and the fee structure (step 5).

title: Buying NSE shares step-by-step
heading: Step 3: Account opening and KYC
text:
Opening a brokerage account involves standard "Know Your Customer" (KYC) requirements — generally a national ID or passport, KRA PIN, passport-sized photo, and proof of address. This is the same kind of documentation required to open a bank account, and exists to comply with anti-money-laundering regulations across the financial sector.

Once approved, your CDS account is opened as part of this process, linked to your broker.

title: Buying NSE shares step-by-step
heading: Step 4: Fund your account
text: Once your account is set up, you transfer money into it — typically via bank transfer or, increasingly, M-Pesa, depending on the broker's platform. This money sits in your trading account, ready to be used to buy shares.

title: Buying NSE shares step-by-step
heading: Step 5: Understand the costs
text:
Buying and selling shares isn't free — there are a few layers of cost:

- **Brokerage commission** — a fee charged by your broker for executing the trade, often a percentage of the transaction value (sometimes with a minimum amount).
- **CDS/CDSC fees** — small fees related to the depository system itself.
- **NSE and regulatory fees** — small additional charges that fund the exchange and capital markets regulation.

These add up to a small percentage of each trade — meaning very small, frequent trades can see a meaningful chunk of the amount go to fees rather than the actual shares. This is one reason why a longer-term, less-frequent approach to buying shares is generally more cost-efficient than frequent small trades — separate from any view on which shares to hold.

title: Buying NSE shares step-by-step
heading: Step 6: Placing an order
text: With a funded account, you place an order through your broker's app or platform (or by phone/in-person with some brokers), specifying the company (by its ticker/code), the number of shares, and the price you're willing to pay (or "market price" to transact at the current available price). If a matching order exists on the other side, the trade executes — subject to the exchange's trading hours and rules.

title: Buying NSE shares step-by-step
heading: What happens after
text: Once a trade settles, the shares appear in your CDS account, linked to you. If the company pays **dividends** (distributions of profit to shareholders), these are typically paid directly to you — often to the bank or mobile money account linked to your brokerage account — based on how many shares you hold on the relevant date.

title: Buying NSE shares step-by-step
heading: The honest summary
text: The *mechanics* — CDS account, licensed broker, KYC, funding, costs, placing orders — are genuinely accessible to an everyday saver in Kenya, more so than a decade ago thanks to mobile-first brokers. The *harder* part — deciding what, when, and how much to buy, and how that fits into your broader financial picture — is a different question entirely, one this lesson deliberately doesn't answer, because the honest answer is "it depends on you," not "it depends on the stock."

title: Buying NSE shares step-by-step
heading: Ask Orchestra-Core
text: If a step in this process is unclear — what KYC documents typically involve, how CDS accounts work, how to think about brokerage fees relative to trade size — Ask Orchestra-Core can walk through it in more detail, as a process explainer, not as guidance on what or when to buy.

title: T-Bills via CBK DhowCSD
text: > **Before we start:** this lesson explains what Treasury Bills are and how to access them — it's not a suggestion that T-Bills are the right place for your money. Where to keep savings depends on your own goals, how soon you might need the money, and your overall plan (see Money Basics, Module 4 on emergency funds).

id: kenya-money/04-tbills-dhowcsd#What a Treasury Bill actually is
title: T-Bills via CBK DhowCSD
heading: What a Treasury Bill actually is
text:
When the Kenyan government needs to borrow money for the short term, it issues **Treasury Bills (T-Bills)** — essentially, an IOU from the government to whoever buys it. T-Bills come in three standard tenors: **91-day, 182-day, and 364-day** (roughly 3, 6, and 12 months).

Because the government is the borrower, T-Bills are generally considered among the lowest-**default**-risk investments available domestically — the government is highly unlikely to simply fail to pay. (This doesn't mean "no risk at all" — more on that below.)

id: kenya-money/04-tbills-dhowcsd#How the return works: it's a discount, not "interest" in the usual sense
title: T-Bills via CBK DhowCSD
heading: How the return works: it's a discount, not "interest" in the usual sense
text:
T-Bills don't pay periodic interest the way a savings account does. Instead, they work on a **discount** basis: you pay *less* than the face value upfront, and receive the *full face value* when the bill matures. The difference is your return.

For example (illustrative numbers only): if a 91-day T-Bill has a face value of KES 100,000 and you buy it at a discount rate that results in a purchase price of KES 97,500, you'd receive KES 100,000 at maturity — a gain of KES 2,500 over 91 days on your KES 97,500 outlay.

To compare this to an annual interest rate (so you can compare it to, say, a SACCO dividend or savings account rate), the discount is annualized — this is the "yield" you'll see quoted. The actual discount rate and resulting yield are determined at auction, based on demand from all bidders — they change from auction to auction depending on market conditions, so there's no fixed number to memorize.

id: kenya-money/04-tbills-dhowcsd#How to access T-Bills directly: DhowCSD
title: T-Bills via CBK DhowCSD
heading: How to access T-Bills directly: DhowCSD
text:
Historically, buying T-Bills directly required going through a commercial bank as an agent. The Central Bank of Kenya's **DhowCSD** platform (dhowcsd.centralbank.go.ke) allows individuals to register and bid for government securities — including T-Bills and Treasury Bonds — directly, without a bank intermediary.

The general process:

1. **Register** on the DhowCSD platform, which involves providing identification details and linking a bank account (for both funding purchases and receiving proceeds at maturity).
2. **Place a bid** during an auction window — auctions happen on a regular schedule (typically weekly for T-Bills), announced in advance.
3. **Choose a bid type** — broadly, a "competitive" bid (you specify the rate you're willing to accept, with the risk it's rejected if too far from the eventually accepted rate) or a "non-competitive" bid (you accept whatever the weighted-average rate from the auction turns out to be — generally simpler for individual investors who aren't trying to optimize the rate).
4. **Funds are debited** from your linked account for successful bids, and the face value is credited back at maturity.

There's a **minimum investment amount** for T-Bills via DhowCSD, which has historically been set in increments (e.g., minimums around KES 50,000-100,000 depending on the instrument) — but minimums and rules can be updated by CBK, so checking the current requirements on the platform itself before assuming a figure is the reliable approach.

id: kenya-money/04-tbills-dhowcsd#The risk that *does* exist: it's not "zero risk"
title: T-Bills via CBK DhowCSD
heading: The risk that *does* exist: it's not "zero risk"
text:
"Government securities are low risk" usually refers specifically to **default risk** — the government paying you back. But two other things are worth understanding:

- **Inflation risk** — if inflation is running higher than your T-Bill's yield, your money is growing in shilling terms but losing purchasing power in real terms. A T-Bill yielding 10% when inflation is 8% has a "real" return closer to 2% — still positive, but much smaller than the headline number suggests.
- **Liquidity** — your money is committed for the bill's tenor (91/182/364 days). While there is a secondary market where government securities can sometimes be sold before maturity, this isn't as instant or guaranteed as withdrawing from a savings account.

id: kenya-money/04-tbills-dhowcsd#How T-Bills compare to SACCOs and money market funds
title: T-Bills via CBK DhowCSD
heading: How T-Bills compare to SACCOs and money market funds
text:
All three are ways of putting savings to work with relatively low risk, but with different tradeoffs:

- **T-Bills (via DhowCSD)**: direct exposure to government rates, fixed term, minimum investment requirement, return known at purchase (as a discount), no intermediary fees beyond the platform itself.
- **SACCO deposits**: dividends often competitive or higher, but paid annually and dependent on the SACCO's performance — not fixed or guaranteed in advance like a T-Bill's discount rate.
- **Money market funds (MMFs)**: professionally managed pools that often invest in a mix of T-Bills, bonds, and bank deposits — lower minimums than direct T-Bills, more liquidity (often a few days to withdraw), but with a management fee, and returns that fluctuate with the underlying portfolio.

None of these is universally "better" — they suit different combinations of amount available, time horizon, and how much hands-on management someone wants.

title: T-Bills via CBK DhowCSD
heading: Ask Orchestra-Core
text: If you want to understand how a specific T-Bill yield translates into an actual return on a specific amount of money, or how the DhowCSD registration process generally works, Ask Orchestra-Core can walk through the calculations and process — always pointing back to CBK's own platform for current rates, minimums, and auction calendars, since these change regularly.

title: What money actually is
text:
Imagine you're a farmer with a sack of maize, and what you actually need is a goat. You find someone with a goat — but he doesn't want maize, he wants a pair of shoes. Now you need to find someone with shoes who wants maize, trade for the shoes, then trade the shoes for the goat. Maybe that chain exists. Usually it doesn't. The trade just dies.

Economists call this the **double coincidence of wants** — for a trade to happen without money, each person has to want exactly what the other has, at exactly the same time. It sounds like a minor inconvenience. It's actually the reason every complex economy on earth eventually invented money.

id: 1/1-1-what-money-actually-is#Money is a story everyone agreed to believe
title: What money actually is
heading: Money is a story everyone agreed to believe
text:
Money is the workaround. Instead of needing the goat-owner to want your maize, you just need him to trust that whatever you hand him can later be exchanged for the shoes he actually wants. That "whatever" doesn't need value of its own — it just needs everyone downstream to keep accepting it.

A KES 1,000 note is paper and ink. It costs the Central Bank of Kenya a few shillings to print. The only reason it buys you anything is that everyone — the shopkeeper, the matatu driver, the landlord — has agreed it will keep working tomorrow. That agreement, and nothing else, is what money is.

id: 1/1-1-what-money-actually-is#A short history of solving the same problem
title: What money actually is
heading: A short history of solving the same problem
text:
Long before notes, societies used **commodity money** — things everyone already wanted, so they doubled as currency. In pre-colonial Kenya and across Africa that meant cattle, cowrie shells, salt, and iron hoes; in parts of Baringo, people's unit of account was literally "one hut" (worth three rupees in colonial coin). Cattle couldn't be split for change and shells could flood the market if someone found a new source — so commodity money had a ceiling.

The next leap was **stamped coins**, starting in the kingdom of Lydia (modern-day Turkey) around the 7th century BC. A government stamp turned a lump of metal that had to be weighed into a token that could simply be counted — a guarantee of value from an authority, not the metal itself. China went further roughly six centuries before Europe did, issuing the world's first government paper money — the *jiaozi* — in Sichuan in the 11th century. Both innovations rest on the same trick: replace something with intrinsic value with a *symbol* everyone trusts, because a symbol is lighter, more divisible, and easier to guarantee.

title: What money actually is
heading: Kenya's own money story
text:
Kenya's currency has gone through this same evolution in living memory. Coastal trade once ran on the Maria Theresa Thaler, then the British India rupee. In 1921 the East African shilling replaced it across Kenya, Uganda, and Tanganyika. Kenya only got its own currency after independence — the first Kenya shilling notes were issued on 1 July 1966, carrying Mzee Jomo Kenyatta's portrait, with coins following in 1967. Today's notes carry no individual's portrait at all — by constitutional design — and instead depict things like agriculture, governance, and the Big Five.

Then, in March 2007, Kenya skipped a step the rest of the world took decades to take: Safaricom launched M-Pesa, and money on a basic phone became normal here before it was normal almost anywhere else.

title: What money actually is
heading: The three jobs money does
text:
Whatever form it takes — cattle, coins, paper, or e-value on a phone — money is defined by three jobs, not by what it's made of:

**A medium of exchange.** It lets you buy a mandazi without needing the seller to want anything you happen to own. It collapses the double-coincidence problem down to a single trade.

**A store of value.** It lets you sell maize in March and spend the proceeds in July, instead of watching the maize itself rot. It's not a perfect store — inflation slowly eats it — but it's far more liquid than crops or cattle.

**A unit of account.** It's the common ruler that lets you compare a haircut to a bag of sugar. Without a shared price tag, you can't budget, and markets can't function.

id: 1/1-1-what-money-actually-is#M-Pesa is the clearest proof of all of this
title: What money actually is
heading: M-Pesa is the clearest proof of all of this
text:
Here's the part most people never stop to think about: is the money in your M-Pesa wallet *real* money, or just numbers on Safaricom's servers?

It's real, and the mechanism is exactly the trust system this whole lesson has been describing. When you load cash with an M-Pesa agent, that cash doesn't vanish into Safaricom's accounts — by law, it goes into trust accounts held at regulated commercial banks, fully matching the e-value on your phone, shilling for shilling. CGAP, the group that studies financial inclusion, puts it plainly: M-Pesa balances are "100% backed by the pooled accounts held in commercial banks" and held in a trust "out of reach from Safaricom" — so even if Safaricom collapsed tomorrow, your balance wouldn't. A physical KES 1,000 note, an M-Pesa balance, and a bank account balance are the same currency in three different costumes, all convertible into each other at par.

That's the whole lesson in one example: money was never about the paper, the coin, or the phone screen. It was always about who you trust to honor it later.

id: 1/1-1-what-money-actually-is#When that trust breaks, money can die
title: What money actually is
heading: When that trust breaks, money can die
text:
Trust isn't automatic — it has to be earned and protected, and history has a few brutal examples of what happens when it isn't. In Zimbabwe, between 2007 and 2009, the government printed money to cover spending it couldn't tax or borrow its way out of. Prices doubled roughly every 24.7 hours at the peak. By January 2009, the central bank issued a **Z$100,000,000,000,000 note — one hundred trillion Zimbabwean dollars** — worth about US$30 the day it was released, and worth essentially nothing soon after. People stopped trusting the currency entirely and switched to US dollars.

The Kenya shilling has never come close to that. It loses value gradually through ordinary inflation — the kind that makes KES 1,000 buy less this year than last — but never catastrophically, because the Central Bank of Kenya is constitutionally independent and targets inflation within a 2.5–7.5% band rather than printing without limit to cover government spending. When the shilling came under real pressure in 2022–2023 — falling past KES 160 to the dollar in January 2024 — the CBK responded by raising interest rates to a 12-year high and Kenya secured a new Eurobond and IMF support; the shilling recovered to around 129 within months. That response is precisely the discipline that separates "your savings lose a bit of value over time" from "your savings become wallpaper."

id: 1/1-1-what-money-actually-is#Why this matters for your money right now
title: What money actually is
heading: Why this matters for your money right now
text:
Because money is a representation of value and not value itself, keeping all your wealth in cash is a choice with a real cost: inflation means the same number of shillings buys a little less every year. A KES 1,000 note from 2010 buys roughly half of what it used to. The note looks identical. Its purchasing power didn't survive.

This is why people who build wealth hold assets — land, businesses, shares — rather than stacks of cash. Cash is the vehicle that moves you between assets, not a destination in itself. Everything else in this course builds on that one idea.

title: What money actually is
heading: Key takeaways
text:
- Money has no inherent value — it works purely because people agree it will keep working tomorrow.
- It exists to solve one problem: the double coincidence of wants that makes barter impractical.
- It does three jobs: medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account.
- M-Pesa is real money, not a workaround — every shilling of e-value is backed 1:1 in regulated bank trust accounts.
- Trust can fail catastrophically (Zimbabwe's 100-trillion-dollar note) — Kenya avoids this because the CBK is independent and disciplined, not because shillings are special paper.

title: What money actually is
heading: Ask Orchestra-Core
text:
- "How exactly does M-Pesa keep my money safe if Safaricom isn't a bank?"
- "Why did the Kenya shilling hit a record low in 2024, and how did it recover?"
- "What's the difference between ordinary inflation and hyperinflation?"

title: What money actually is
heading: What's next
text: Next up: **How banks create money** — the mechanism is even stranger than this one. Banks don't just store the money you deposit. They create new money every time they issue a loan, and that single fact runs the entire financial system.

title: How banks create money
text:
Most people think banks are like giant safes. You deposit KES 10,000. They store it. Somebody else comes along and borrows some of it. Simple.

That's not what happens. Banks create money out of thin air — legally, deliberately, and constantly. Understanding this mechanic is one of the most important things you can know about how the financial world actually works.

title: How banks create money
heading: The vault that's mostly empty
text:
When you deposit KES 10,000 at Equity Bank, Equity doesn't lock that money away for you. They keep a small fraction — maybe 10-20% — as a reserve, in case you and other depositors want to withdraw. The rest they lend out to someone else.

So your KES 10,000 deposit becomes KES 8,000 lent to a small business owner who needed working capital. That business owner deposits the KES 8,000 into their account at KCB. KCB keeps a reserve and lends out, say, KES 6,400 to someone who needs a car loan. That person's seller deposits KES 6,400 into Co-operative Bank. And it continues.

Your original KES 10,000 has, through this chain, become roughly KES 35,000-40,000 of money in circulation. Nobody printed extra notes. The central bank didn't authorise anything new. The bank system simply lent and re-lent, and each cycle created a new deposit.

This is called **fractional reserve banking** — banks only hold a fraction of deposits as actual reserves. The rest is lent into existence as new money.

title: How banks create money
heading: Why banks can do this
text:
You might wonder: is this legal? Is it ethical? The answer is yes to the first question and contested on the second, but it's been the standard model globally for centuries.

It works as long as not everyone tries to withdraw at the same time. Your KES 10,000 is "there" in the sense that you can withdraw it. But it's also powering KES 30,000+ of economic activity simultaneously. If every depositor at a bank tried to withdraw on the same day — a "bank run" — the bank collapses, because the vault is mostly empty.

This is why governments insure bank deposits (in Kenya, up to KES 500,000 per account via the Kenya Deposit Insurance Corporation). The insurance exists because without it, the fractional reserve system would be perpetually vulnerable to panics.

title: How banks create money
heading: The Central Bank of Kenya's role
text:
The CBK sits above this whole system. It sets the reserve requirement — how much commercial banks must hold back. It sets the base interest rate — the rate at which banks borrow from the CBK itself when they need reserves. When the CBK raises interest rates, borrowing becomes more expensive, banks lend less, and less new money enters the economy. When it cuts rates, borrowing is cheaper, banks lend more, and more money circulates.

This is how monetary policy works. The CBK doesn't need to print money to stimulate the economy. It can simply lower interest rates and watch banks multiply existing deposits into more loans, more spending, and more economic activity.

When you hear "the CBK raised rates," it means: credit is getting more expensive, loans will shrink, less money will be created by banks, and the economy will slow slightly. Usually this is done to fight inflation. When prices are rising too fast, reducing the money supply slows spending down and pulls prices back.

title: How banks create money
heading: What this means for you
text:
Three things directly affect your life from this understanding.

First, **interest rates on your loans and savings are connected to this system**. When the CBK raises rates, your mobile loan interest goes up. Your mortgage repayment goes up. Your savings account interest also goes up — though banks are much faster to raise what you pay than what you earn.

Second, **keeping money in a bank is not the same as keeping it safe**. Your deposit is partially lent out immediately. The bank owes you KES 10,000 but doesn't have KES 10,000 sitting in a vault. It has the promise of KES 10,000 — backed by the assets of the bank and the government's deposit insurance. That's usually fine. But it's not zero risk.

Third, **this is why "tight money" and "loose money" matter**. When credit is easy, businesses borrow, expand, hire, and the economy grows. When credit tightens, growth slows. Every time you see a headline about CBK interest rate decisions, this is what's really being decided: how much new money will the banking system create this month?

title: How banks create money
heading: Key takeaways
text:
- Banks don't store your full deposit — they lend most of it out, creating new money in the process.
- This is fractional reserve banking and it's legal, standard, and global.
- Your KES 10,000 deposit can power KES 30,000-40,000+ of economic activity across multiple banks.
- The CBK controls how aggressively this happens by setting interest rates and reserve requirements.
- Kenya Deposit Insurance Corporation covers up to KES 500,000 per account if a bank fails.

title: How banks create money
heading: What's next
text: You now understand what money is and how it multiplies through banks. Next: **The 50/30/20 rule** — the simplest budgeting framework ever designed, and why most Kenyans never use it but should.

title: The 50/30/20 rule
text:
Budgeting sounds complicated. Spreadsheets, categories, tracking every mandazi. Most people try it for two weeks, give up, and go back to spending until the money runs out.

The 50/30/20 rule is different. It's not a spreadsheet. It's a single decision made once, applied to every paycheck automatically. It's so simple that even people who hate budgeting can follow it.

title: The 50/30/20 rule
heading: The idea
text:
Split every shilling you earn into three buckets — no more, no less.

**50% — Needs.** Half your income goes to things you cannot live without. Rent. Food. Transport to work. Electricity. Phone airtime. NHIF contributions. These are non-negotiable. If you earn KES 40,000 a month, KES 20,000 covers your survival.

**30% — Wants.** Three-tenths goes to things that improve your life but aren't survival. Eating out. New clothes beyond basics. Entertainment. A nicer phone than the one you technically need. DStv. These are real — everyone has wants, and pretending you don't only leads to binge spending later.

**20% — Savings and debt repayment.** The final fifth goes to your future self. SACCO contributions. Emergency fund. Investment. If you have mobile loans, credit card debt, or any outstanding balance, this bucket also services that debt — clearing it as fast as possible, because debt costs more than savings earns.

id: 1/1-3-the-50-30-20-rule#What this looks like on a Kenyan salary
title: The 50/30/20 rule
heading: What this looks like on a Kenyan salary
text:
Let's make this concrete. You've just started a job in Nairobi earning a net salary of KES 55,000 a month after PAYE and NSSF deductions.

| Bucket | Percentage | Monthly amount |
|---|---|---|
| Needs | 50% | KES 27,500 |
| Wants | 30% | KES 16,500 |
| Savings / debt | 20% | KES 11,000 |

Your KES 27,500 needs bucket covers: rent in a decent Nairobi estate (KES 12,000-18,000), transport (KES 3,000-5,000 matatu or fuel), food basics (KES 6,000-8,000), electricity and water (KES 1,500-2,500), phone bundles (KES 500-1,000). It's tight but workable if you're not paying rent in Westlands.

Your KES 16,500 wants bucket is real money. That covers dinner out a few times a month, a streaming service, occasional shopping, and social life.

Your KES 11,000 savings bucket, invested consistently in a money market fund earning around 12% annually, becomes roughly KES 190,000 in 18 months and KES 440,000 in three years — without you doing anything except not touching it.

title: The 50/30/20 rule
heading: The Kenya reality check
text:
The 50/30/20 rule was designed for American incomes and American costs. In Kenya, some adjustments are honest and necessary.

If you earn KES 25,000 net in Nairobi, 50% needs is KES 12,500. Rent alone in most decent areas starts at KES 8,000-10,000. The math gets uncomfortable fast. In this case, reduce wants aggressively — perhaps 60/15/25 — and treat increasing income as urgent. The rule doesn't override reality; it gives you a target to aim toward.

If you're sending money home — parents, siblings, family obligations — that comes from your needs bucket, not your savings. Family obligations are not wants. They're real costs that have to be accounted for honestly, or the budget will break every month and you'll stop trying.

title: The 50/30/20 rule
heading: How to actually implement this
text:
Don't track. Automate.

The day your salary arrives, immediately move 20% to a separate M-Pesa wallet, SACCO account, or savings account. Before you buy anything. Before you pay rent. Move it first.

This is called **paying yourself first**, and it's the single most important habit in personal finance. Once it's in the savings account, it's psychologically gone. You budget the remaining 80% for needs and wants. Most people try to save what's left after spending — there's never anything left.

Set up a standing order with your bank or a recurring M-Pesa to SACCO transfer on the same day as your payroll date. Automation makes this frictionless.

title: The 50/30/20 rule
heading: Key takeaways
text:
- 50% of net income to needs, 30% to wants, 20% to savings and debt repayment.
- Paying yourself first — moving savings before anything else — is what makes this work in practice.
- For lower Nairobi salaries, adjust to 60/15/25 or 65/10/25 but never abandon the savings bucket entirely.
- Family obligations count as needs, not wants — budget them honestly.
- Automate the 20% transfer on payday so willpower isn't required.

title: The 50/30/20 rule
heading: What's next
text: You have a budget. But here's the thing — even a budget that works perfectly will lose to one enemy: time. Next: **Compound interest — the eighth wonder of the world**, and why starting to save at 22 beats starting at 35 by a catastrophic margin.

title: Compound interest — the eighth wonder
text:
Albert Einstein allegedly called compound interest the eighth wonder of the world. Whether he actually said it or not, the idea is correct. Compound interest is the closest thing to a financial superpower that an ordinary person can access — and almost nobody starts using it early enough.

Here's the concept in one sentence: **you earn interest on your interest, not just your original money.** That feedback loop, given enough time, produces numbers that feel impossible.

title: Compound interest — the eighth wonder
heading: The numbers that change minds
text:
Let's say two people, Amina and Baraka, both want to retire comfortably. Both earn reasonable Nairobi salaries. The only difference is when they start saving.

Amina starts at 22. She puts KES 5,000 a month into a money market fund earning 12% annually. At 35 — just 13 years later — she stops contributing entirely. She puts in nothing more for the rest of her working life. Total contributed: KES 780,000.

Baraka starts at 35. He also puts KES 5,000 a month into the same fund at the same 12% rate. He keeps going until he's 65 — 30 full years of contributions. Total contributed: KES 1,800,000.

At 65, who has more?

Amina: **approximately KES 24 million.**
Baraka: **approximately KES 17 million.**

Amina contributed less than half of what Baraka did. She stopped saving 30 years before he did. She still ends up with more — by KES 7 million. The only thing she had that he didn't was a 13-year head start.

This is compound interest. Time is the ingredient that no amount of money can buy back.

title: Compound interest — the eighth wonder
heading: Why it works — the mechanics
text:
Simple interest earns returns only on your original amount. If you deposit KES 100,000 at 10% simple interest, you earn KES 10,000 every year. After 10 years: KES 200,000. Straightforward.

Compound interest earns returns on your original amount plus all previously earned interest. Year 1: KES 100,000 earns KES 10,000 → total KES 110,000. Year 2: KES 110,000 earns KES 11,000 → total KES 121,000. Year 3: KES 121,000 earns KES 12,100 → total KES 133,100.

The growth accelerates. By year 20, your KES 100,000 has become KES 672,000 — without a single additional deposit. The money earned money, and that money earned more money.

Most Kenyan money market funds compound daily or monthly, which is even more powerful than annual compounding. The CIC Money Market Fund, Britam, Sanlam, and others have historically delivered 10-14% annual returns, though past returns don't guarantee future ones.

title: Compound interest — the eighth wonder
heading: The rule of 72
text:
Here's a useful shortcut: divide 72 by your interest rate to estimate how many years it takes to double your money.

At 12% annual return: 72 ÷ 12 = **6 years to double your money.**

KES 100,000 at 22 → KES 200,000 at 28 → KES 400,000 at 34 → KES 800,000 at 40 → KES 1,600,000 at 46 → KES 3,200,000 at 52 → KES 6,400,000 at 58.

Starting with KES 100,000 at 22 and never adding another shilling, you can reach KES 6.4 million by 58 at 12% returns. That's the power of time. That's compound interest at work.

At 10%: money doubles every 7.2 years.
At 15%: money doubles every 4.8 years.

The higher the rate, the faster the doubling. But even modest rates produce extraordinary results with enough time.

title: Compound interest — the eighth wonder
heading: The enemy: debt compound interest
text:
Compound interest cuts both ways. When it works for you as a saver, it's a superpower. When it works against you as a borrower, it's a trap.

Mobile loans in Kenya — Fuliza, KCB M-Pesa, and others — charge interest rates that can reach 60-100%+ annually when annualised. At 100% compound interest, a debt doubles in one year. A KES 10,000 loan at that rate becomes KES 20,000 owed in 12 months without repaying a shilling.

This is exactly what happens to people stuck in the mobile loan cycle. They borrow KES 2,000 for rent. They can't repay it plus interest on payday, so they borrow more to cover it. The debt compounds faster than their income grows, and the hole gets deeper every month.

Understanding compound interest means understanding both why to save early and why to destroy debt fast — especially high-interest debt. The mathematics is identical; only the direction changes.

title: Compound interest — the eighth wonder
heading: Starting with very little
text:
You don't need KES 100,000 to start. Money market funds in Kenya accept as little as KES 1,000 to open. Ziidi (M-Pesa), Sanlam, ICEA Lion, and others are accessible via phone.

KES 500 a month from age 20, at 12% annually, becomes approximately KES 3.2 million by age 60. KES 500. The same cost as a lunch out. Every month for 40 years. KES 3.2 million.

Nobody is too poor to start. They might be too uninformed to start — and that's exactly what this lesson is for.

title: Compound interest — the eighth wonder
heading: Key takeaways
text:
- Compound interest earns returns on your interest, not just your original money — producing exponential, not linear, growth.
- Time is the most powerful ingredient. Starting 10 years earlier beats contributing twice as much.
- The Rule of 72: divide 72 by your interest rate to find how many years to double your money.
- At 12%, money doubles every 6 years.
- Compound interest also works in reverse on debt — high-interest mobile loans compound against you just as fast.
- Even KES 500-1,000 a month invested at 20 can produce millions by retirement.

title: Compound interest — the eighth wonder
heading: What's next
text: You now understand the time value of money. But not all debt is bad — some is how people build wealth. Next: **Good debt vs bad debt** — and the difference between borrowing that builds you up and borrowing that slowly destroys you.

title: Good debt vs bad debt
text:
"Avoid debt" is advice you'll hear constantly. It's also wrong — or at least incomplete.

The wealthiest people on the planet use debt constantly and deliberately. They borrow to buy property. They take loans to start businesses. They use credit to fund investments that return more than the interest they pay. Debt, used correctly, is a tool that multiplies your ability to build wealth.

The problem isn't debt. It's bad debt. And knowing the difference is one of the most important financial skills you can develop.

id: 1/1-5-good-debt-vs-bad-debt#Good debt: borrowing to create more than you borrow
title: Good debt vs bad debt
heading: Good debt: borrowing to create more than you borrow
text:
Good debt has two characteristics. The cost of borrowing (the interest rate) is lower than the return you expect from what you're buying. And what you're buying either appreciates in value or generates income.

**A mortgage on a property** is the classic example. You borrow KES 4 million at 13% from a bank to buy a house. You rent it out for KES 40,000 a month — KES 480,000 a year. Your mortgage repayments are roughly KES 45,000 a month — KES 540,000 a year. You're paying slightly more than you earn in rent initially, but the property is also appreciating — in Nairobi, property values have historically grown 7-12% annually. Over 15-20 years, you own an asset worth multiples of what you paid, funded largely by rent and a loan. That's good debt working.

**A student loan for a marketable qualification** can be good debt. You borrow to fund education that increases your earning capacity by more than the interest costs over time. A HELB loan at relatively low interest funding a degree that adds KES 20,000/month to your income for 40 years is good debt.

**A business loan to expand a profitable operation** — borrowing KES 500,000 at 18% to buy inventory that generates KES 150,000 profit per cycle — is good debt if the maths works out.

title: Good debt vs bad debt
heading: Bad debt: borrowing to consume
text:
Bad debt funds things that lose value immediately and generate no return — and typically at very high interest rates.

**Mobile loans for daily expenses.** Borrowing on Fuliza to buy food, pay rent, or cover shortfalls is bad debt. The interest rate is extremely high when annualised, and what you're buying disappears immediately. You're paying more for something that's already gone.

**Buy-now-pay-later for electronics or clothes.** Your new phone loses 30% of its value the moment you leave the shop. Paying 20%+ interest on a depreciating asset doubles the damage.

**Credit to fund a lifestyle you can't afford.** Borrowing to maintain appearances — the car you can't afford, the holiday that empties your account, the outfit for someone's wedding — is the most destructive form of bad debt. The social pressure to spend beyond your means is real in Kenya, and so are the consequences.

title: Good debt vs bad debt
heading: The grey area: car loans
text:
Car loans are interesting because they sit between good and bad depending on circumstance.

A car depreciates — it loses value immediately and continuously. In that sense, a car loan is bad debt. But if the car enables you to earn more — a boda boda loan for a business, a vehicle needed to access a better-paying job that public transport can't reach, or a commercial vehicle for business — then the income generated can justify the cost. The test: does owning this car make you more money than the loan costs you?

If the answer is yes, it's potentially acceptable debt. If it's a lifestyle car funded by debt just to have one, it's bad debt wrapped in monthly payments.

title: Good debt vs bad debt
heading: How to evaluate any debt
text:
Before taking any loan, ask four questions.

What is the true annual interest rate? Mobile loans often advertise daily rates — 1.5% per day sounds small. Compounded annually, it's over 700%. Always annualise the rate before agreeing.

What am I buying with this money? Does it appreciate, generate income, or immediately lose value and disappear?

Is the return on what I'm buying higher than the interest I'm paying? If you're borrowing at 15% and the investment returns 25%, the maths works. If you're borrowing at 20% for something that returns nothing, you're losing 20% a year.

Can I genuinely service this debt without damaging other parts of my financial life? Debt that eats into your savings, emergency fund, or family obligations isn't good debt regardless of what you buy with it.

title: Good debt vs bad debt
heading: Key takeaways
text:
- Good debt buys things that appreciate or generate income at a rate higher than the interest you pay.
- Bad debt funds consumption — things that lose value immediately and generate no return.
- Mortgages, business loans, and education loans can be good debt. Mobile loans for daily expenses almost never are.
- Always annualise interest rates — 1.5% per day is over 700% annually.
- Before any loan, ask: what does this buy me, and does the return beat the interest?

title: Good debt vs bad debt
heading: What's next
text: You understand debt. Now the silent destroyer: **Inflation — why your KES 1,000 shrinks every year** even when you haven't spent it.

title: Inflation — why your KES 1,000 shrinks
text:
Think about what KES 1,000 bought your parents in 2000. A full week of groceries. Today that same KES 1,000 buys maybe a day's worth of basics. The note looks identical. The number is the same. But the purchasing power has quietly, steadily evaporated.

This is inflation. And it's the most important economic force most people never properly understand.

title: Inflation — why your KES 1,000 shrinks
heading: What inflation actually is
text:
Inflation is the general rise in prices across an economy over time. Not one thing getting more expensive — everything. Bread, rent, matatu fares, school fees, airtime. When the average price level rises by 8% in a year, that's 8% inflation.

The most common measure is the Consumer Price Index (CPI) — a basket of goods and services that represents typical household spending. The Kenya National Bureau of Statistics (KNBS) tracks this monthly. When the CPI rises, prices are rising. Kenya has experienced inflation rates ranging from around 3% to over 10% in recent years depending on global oil prices, food supply, and the exchange rate.

title: Inflation — why your KES 1,000 shrinks
heading: What causes inflation
text:
Several things push prices up, and understanding the causes helps you anticipate them.

**Too much money chasing too few goods.** When the government prints money or banks create too much credit, more money exists but the same amount of goods does. Sellers can charge more because buyers have more money available. Prices rise.

**Import costs rising.** Kenya imports a lot — oil, machinery, some food. When the KES weakens against the dollar, everything imported becomes more expensive in shilling terms. A weaker KES means higher petrol prices, which means higher transport costs for every good in the country. The 2022-2023 period saw significant inflation in Kenya partly for this reason.

**Supply shocks.** When the supply of something essential drops suddenly — a drought cutting food production, a global shipping disruption — prices spike even if nothing changed with the money supply. Covid-19 created supply shocks globally, and Kenya felt them.

**Demand pull.** When an economy grows strongly and everyone has more money to spend, demand for goods and services rises faster than supply can match. Prices are pulled upward.

title: Inflation — why your KES 1,000 shrinks
heading: How inflation affects your money specifically
text:
If you have KES 100,000 sitting in a standard bank account earning 3% annual interest, and inflation is 7%, your real return is negative 4%. The number in your account grows slightly. But what it can buy shrinks. You're losing ground while feeling like you're standing still.

This is called **negative real returns** — when the nominal (stated) interest rate is lower than inflation. It's the silent tax on savers who park money in low-yield accounts.

The mathematics are unforgiving over time. At 7% annual inflation, prices double in roughly 10 years. Something that costs KES 5,000 today costs KES 10,000 in 2036. Your savings need to at least keep pace with inflation to maintain their value — and ideally beat it to actually grow.

title: Inflation — why your KES 1,000 shrinks
heading: What beats inflation in Kenya
text:
Cash savings accounts at most Kenyan banks earn 3-7% interest. With inflation often in that same range, you're barely treading water.

Money market funds have historically returned 10-14% in Kenya — above inflation in most years. Your money grows in real terms. Government bonds and Treasury bills offer similar or slightly lower returns with government-backed security.

NSE equities are volatile but have historically returned more than inflation over long periods. Property — again over long periods — has also beaten inflation in most Nairobi locations.

The pattern is consistent: assets that produce income or appreciate tend to beat inflation. Cash sitting still tends to lose to it.

title: Inflation — why your KES 1,000 shrinks
heading: The positive side: inflation and debt
text:
There is one group that actually benefits from inflation: borrowers with fixed-rate debt.

If you took a mortgage at 14% fixed for 20 years and inflation runs at 10% for the next decade, the real cost of your debt is only 4%. Your salary rises with inflation. Your fixed mortgage payment stays the same. In real terms, your debt is shrinking while your income grows. This is why property investors often like a moderate amount of inflation — it erodes the real value of their mortgage while their asset value and rental income rises.

This is also why governments sometimes find inflation convenient. National debt becomes easier to service when inflation is running high and tax revenues are rising with inflation even though the debt face value stays fixed.

title: Inflation — why your KES 1,000 shrinks
heading: Key takeaways
text:
- Inflation is the general rise in prices over time. At 7%, prices double every 10 years.
- Cash savings earning less than inflation lose real purchasing power silently every year.
- The CBK manages inflation through interest rates — raising rates to cool inflation, cutting them to stimulate growth.
- Money market funds (10-14% returns) have historically beaten Kenya's inflation. Basic savings accounts often don't.
- Borrowers with fixed-rate debt can benefit from inflation as it erodes the real value of what they owe.

title: Inflation — why your KES 1,000 shrinks
heading: What's next
text: You now know that cash loses to inflation. Which means you need a safety net before you invest. Next: **The emergency fund — the boring financial move that protects everything else.**

title: The emergency fund — the boring secret
text:
Here's a situation most Kenyans know personally: an unexpected cost arrives — a medical bill, a job loss, a broken phone you need for work, a family emergency — and because there's no cushion anywhere, you borrow. You borrow at high interest from a mobile loan, from a friend, from a SACCO against your shares. The emergency passes but the debt remains. Next month is tighter. The cycle starts.

The emergency fund is the thing that breaks this cycle before it starts. It's the most boring, least glamorous financial move you can make. It's also the one that protects everything else.

title: The emergency fund — the boring secret
heading: What an emergency fund is
text:
An emergency fund is 3-6 months of your essential living expenses saved in a liquid, accessible account — not invested, not in a SACCO where withdrawal takes time, not tied up anywhere. Available within 24-48 hours when you need it.

Essential expenses means: rent, food, transport, utilities, and any commitments you cannot miss. Not wants. Not subscriptions. Just survival costs.

If your essential monthly expenses are KES 25,000, your emergency fund target is KES 75,000 to KES 150,000. That's it. Once you hit that number, this bucket is full. You stop adding to it and redirect that money to investments.

title: The emergency fund — the boring secret
heading: Why liquid matters
text:
Stocks can drop 30% in a month. A SACCO withdrawal often takes 1-3 months for substantial amounts. Chama savings require group agreement. Property can't be sold in a week.

When a genuine emergency hits — a job loss, a sudden medical cost, a critical household breakdown — you need money now, not in three months. The emergency fund lives in a money market fund (accessible in 24-48 hours with most Kenyan providers like Ziidi, CIC, or Sanlam), a savings account, or your M-Pesa. Somewhere liquid. Not in investments.

The point isn't to earn returns on this money. It's to make sure you never have to borrow at high interest rates during a crisis.

title: The emergency fund — the boring secret
heading: Building it when you have nothing
text:
Starting from zero with KES 25,000 expenses and no cushion at all, here's a realistic approach.

Target first: KES 25,000 — one month of expenses. Even one month changes your financial situation dramatically. A single month of cushion means you can handle most everyday emergencies without borrowing.

Save aggressively toward this first milestone. If necessary, temporarily drop wants spending to near zero. No eating out, no entertainment beyond free options. Three to four months of focused saving should get most employed people to one month's expenses.

Then breathe, restore some wants spending, and build toward three months at a slower pace.

title: The emergency fund — the boring secret
heading: What counts as a real emergency
text:
This sounds obvious, but for many people it isn't once the fund exists and temptation arrives.

Real emergencies: sudden medical treatment, unexpected job loss, essential home repair (burst pipe, no water), a required work tool that breaks.

Not emergencies: a sale on something you want, an event you feel social pressure to attend, impulse purchases, anything planned in advance. If you knew about it more than a month ago, it's not an emergency — it's poor planning.

Protect the fund fiercely. The moment it becomes a general savings pot it stops doing its job.

title: The emergency fund — the boring secret
heading: The peace of mind dividend
text:
There's a financial return on an emergency fund that doesn't show up in any calculation: the change in how you live.

When you have three months of expenses saved, the anxiety around money changes fundamentally. A bad month at work doesn't become a crisis. An unexpected bill doesn't spiral. You make better financial decisions generally because you're not operating from scarcity and panic.

People without emergency funds often make terrible financial decisions when emergencies hit — taking out high-interest loans, selling investments at the worst time, making impulsive choices under stress. The fund prevents all of that. Its value is partly financial and partly psychological, and both parts are real.

title: The emergency fund — the boring secret
heading: Key takeaways
text:
- An emergency fund is 3-6 months of essential living expenses in a liquid, accessible account.
- It lives in a money market fund or savings account — not tied up in investments or SACCO.
- The goal is not returns. The goal is avoiding high-interest debt during crises.
- Build to one month first, then three months, then six.
- Protect it: only genuine unexpected emergencies justify withdrawal, not desires or poor planning.

title: The emergency fund — the boring secret
heading: Ask Orchestra-Core
text:
- "What's the best liquid account for an emergency fund in Kenya right now?"
- "I have mobile loan debt and no emergency fund — which should I tackle first?"
- "Help me calculate exactly what my emergency fund target should be."

title: The emergency fund — the boring secret
heading: What's next
text: You have a budget, savings habit, and emergency cushion. The final piece of the foundation: **Net worth — how to actually measure where you stand financially**, and why it's the number that matters more than your salary.

title: Net worth — how to measure yourself
text:
Most people measure their financial life by their salary. "I earn KES 80,000 a month" feels like a statement of financial health. But salary tells you almost nothing useful. It tells you the size of the pipe, not the size of the tank.

Net worth tells you where you actually stand. It's the single number that captures your true financial position — and tracking it over time is the most honest feedback loop you can have.

title: Net worth — how to measure yourself
heading: The formula
text:
Net worth = Everything you own (assets) minus everything you owe (liabilities).

That's it. Two lists. Subtract one from the other.

If you own KES 150,000 in a money market fund, KES 50,000 in SACCO shares, and a laptop worth KES 80,000, your assets total KES 280,000.

If you owe KES 30,000 on a mobile loan, KES 15,000 to a friend, and KES 45,000 in a SACCO loan, your liabilities total KES 90,000.

Net worth: KES 280,000 − KES 90,000 = **KES 190,000.**

title: Net worth — how to measure yourself
heading: What counts as an asset
text:
Assets are things of financial value you own.

**Liquid assets**: money market funds, savings accounts, M-Pesa balance, cash.
**Investment assets**: shares on the NSE, unit trust holdings, Treasury bills you hold.
**SACCO shares and deposits**: the accumulated shares in your SACCO, not the loan facility.
**Property**: if you own land or a house, its estimated current market value.
**Business assets**: the value of inventory, equipment, or receivables if you run a business.

Note what's not usually on the list: your personal phone, clothes, furniture, and most everyday possessions. They have value but negligible resale value and aren't financial assets. Include them only if they're genuinely significant and realisable.

title: Net worth — how to measure yourself
heading: What counts as a liability
text:
Liabilities are everything you owe to anyone.

**Mobile loans**: Fuliza, KCB M-Pesa, branch loans, Tala, any outstanding balance.
**Bank loans**: outstanding loan balances, credit cards.
**SACCO loans**: borrowed against your SACCO shares.
**Chama debt**: if your chama has given you money you haven't contributed to yet.
**Informal debt**: money owed to family, friends, anyone.

Be honest. People consistently undercount their liabilities because informal debt feels different from bank debt. It isn't. It's still money you owe.

title: Net worth — how to measure yourself
heading: What a healthy trajectory looks like
text:
Nobody expects a 22-year-old to have a positive net worth. Student loans, zero savings, a borrowed first deposit — starting life with a negative net worth is normal.

What matters is the direction. Is your net worth going up month after month, year after year? That is the only financial question worth asking consistently.

A salary earner who earns KES 60,000 but spends everything and saves nothing has a stagnant net worth. An earner on KES 35,000 who saves 20% monthly and avoids debt is growing their net worth steadily. In 10 years, the second person is almost certainly in a stronger position despite earning less.

Track your net worth every six months. Write it in a notebook, a spreadsheet, a notes app. Six months of data shows a direction. Three years of data shows a trajectory. That trajectory is your real financial story — not your salary, not your job title, not what you appear to have.

title: Net worth — how to measure yourself
heading: The net worth that lies
text:
One important caveat: net worth can be inflated by assets that are hard to value or unlikely to be realised.

If you list your share of your family's upcountry land at a high estimated value, but that land has never been surveyed, has unclear title, and could never practically be sold, it's not really an asset in the meaningful sense. Count assets at realistic, realisable values.

Similarly, cars depreciate fast. A car bought for KES 2.5 million in 2022 may be worth KES 1.4 million today. List it at today's value, not what you paid.

title: Net worth — how to measure yourself
heading: Key takeaways
text:
- Net worth = assets minus liabilities. It's the single most important financial number you own.
- Salary measures income flow. Net worth measures accumulated wealth. They're completely different things.
- Track net worth every six months. Direction matters more than the current number.
- Be honest about liabilities — including informal debt to family and friends.
- Value assets realistically, not optimistically.

title: How Warren Buffett reads a balance sheet
text: > **Before we start:** this lesson is about *how to read* a financial statement — a skill, like reading a map. It is not a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any investment. The goal is that the news, and conversations about companies, make more sense afterward — not that you act on anything here.

id: smart-money/01-buffett-balance-sheet#What a balance sheet actually is
title: How Warren Buffett reads a balance sheet
heading: What a balance sheet actually is
text:
Every company's balance sheet follows one equation, and everything else is detail:

**Assets = Liabilities + Equity**

In plain terms: everything a company *owns* (assets) was paid for either with money it *owes* (liabilities — loans, unpaid bills) or money that belongs to its *owners* (equity — shareholders' stake). It's a snapshot, at one moment in time, of what a company has and who has a claim on it.

Compare this to an **income statement**, which shows activity *over a period* (revenue, costs, profit for the year). The balance sheet is a photo; the income statement is a video.

id: smart-money/01-buffett-balance-sheet#The lens: businesses as things you'd want to own forever
title: How Warren Buffett reads a balance sheet
heading: The lens: businesses as things you'd want to own forever
text:
A famous long-term investing approach — most associated with Warren Buffett — starts from a simple question: *if you couldn't sell this for ten years, would you still want to own it?* That question changes what you look for in a balance sheet, away from "will the price go up next month" and toward "is this a fundamentally sound business."

A few things this lens pays attention to:

### 1. Debt levels (relative to equity)

A company funded mostly by shareholder money (equity) has more breathing room than one funded mostly by borrowed money (debt) — debt has to be repaid on schedule regardless of how business is going, while equity doesn't. A useful ratio here is **debt-to-equity**: total liabilities divided by total equity. There's no single "good" number — it varies hugely by industry (a bank's balance sheet looks completely different from a software company's) — but a company whose debt has been climbing steadily relative to its equity, without a clear reason, is worth a second look.

### 2. Return on equity (ROE)

ROE = net profit ÷ shareholders' equity. It answers: "for every shilling of owners' money invested in this business, how much profit did it generate?" A consistently high ROE — especially one that doesn't rely on heavy borrowing to inflate it — is often a sign of what's sometimes called a "moat": some durable advantage (a brand, a network effect, a cost advantage) that lets a company earn more on its capital than competitors can.

### 3. Cash vs. reported profit

A company can report a profit on paper while actually running low on cash — for example, if a large portion of "sales" are owed by customers who haven't paid yet (accounts receivable), or if profit includes one-off items that won't recur. Comparing reported net income to actual cash flow from operations is a quick sanity check: are these two numbers telling roughly the same story, or pulling apart?

title: How Warren Buffett reads a balance sheet
heading: A worked illustration
text:
Imagine a fictional company, "Jaza Foods Ltd," with the following (simplified, illustrative) balance sheet:

- Total assets: KES 500 million
- Total liabilities: KES 150 million
- Shareholders' equity: KES 350 million

Debt-to-equity = 150 ÷ 350 ≈ **0.43**. For context, this means the company has roughly 43 cents of liabilities for every shilling of shareholder equity — a relatively conservative position, though "conservative" still depends on the industry.

If Jaza Foods reported net profit of KES 52.5 million for the year, ROE = 52.5 ÷ 350 = **15%**. Whether 15% is "good" depends on what similar companies in the same industry typically achieve — which is why comparison matters more than the raw number in isolation.

id: smart-money/01-buffett-balance-sheet#What this lens deliberately ignores — and why that's also useful
title: How Warren Buffett reads a balance sheet
heading: What this lens deliberately ignores — and why that's also useful
text: This approach tends to downplay short-term price movements, news cycles, and predictions about next quarter. That's not because those things don't matter at all — it's because they're far harder to have a genuine edge in, especially for an individual investor without specialized tools or information. A balance sheet, by contrast, is public, available to everyone at the same time, and doesn't change minute to minute. Learning to read it is a skill that compounds — the more balance sheets you read, the faster you spot what's normal and what's unusual.

id: smart-money/01-buffett-balance-sheet#Why this is education, not a strategy to copy
title: How Warren Buffett reads a balance sheet
heading: Why this is education, not a strategy to copy
text: Notice that nothing above said "therefore buy companies with ROE above X%." Real analysis involves dozens of factors, industry context, management quality, competitive dynamics, and more — and even professional investors who use this exact lens disagree with each other constantly about specific companies. The value of this lesson isn't a checklist for picking stocks. It's that the next time you read that a company's "debt levels are concerning" or its "returns on capital are strong," you'll know roughly what that means and why someone might say it — which is the actual goal: understanding how the conversation works, not winning it.

title: What a 13F filing tells you
text: > **Before we start:** this lesson explains a type of public regulatory filing and the academic research around it, as a case study in market psychology and information. It is not investment advice, and "what a famous investor owns" is never a reason on its own to buy or sell anything.

title: What a 13F filing tells you
heading: What a 13F actually is
text:
In the United States, any institutional investment manager overseeing more than $100 million in certain securities must file a **Form 13F** with the SEC every quarter, listing their holdings — which stocks, how many shares, and their approximate value — as of the end of that quarter.

This requirement exists for transparency: regulators (and the public) get a window into what large investors are doing with their money. Financial media often turn 13F season into headlines — "Famous Investor X buys [company]," "Famous Investor Y exits [company]."

id: smart-money/02-13f-filings#The 45-day lag — the single most important fact about 13Fs
title: What a 13F filing tells you
heading: The 45-day lag — the single most important fact about 13Fs
text:
A 13F covers a *quarter* (three months), and isn't due until **45 days after that quarter ends**. So when a 13F is published, it's describing a snapshot of what a fund owned *up to 45 days ago* — and the fund could have changed its position completely the day after that snapshot was taken, without anyone knowing for months.

Think about what that means: by the time the public sees "Investor X bought Company Y in Q1," it might already be mid-Q2. Investor X could have already sold. The information isn't *wrong* — it's just **old**, and the gap between "true" and "old" is exactly where a lot of misunderstanding happens.

id: smart-money/02-13f-filings#What a 13F shows — and what it can never show
title: What a 13F filing tells you
heading: What a 13F shows — and what it can never show
text:
A 13F shows **long positions in certain US-listed equities and similar instruments**, as of one date, each quarter. That's it. Here's what it does *not* show:

- **Short positions** — bets that a stock will fall. A fund could be massively short something while a 13F shows no hint of it.
- **Derivatives, in most cases** — options and other instruments are often reported in ways that obscure the actual economic bet being made.
- **Cash levels** — a fund sitting 80% in cash, waiting for opportunities, looks identical in a 13F to one that's fully invested, if you only look at what it *does* hold.
- **Non-US holdings, in many cases** — international positions often fall outside 13F reporting entirely.
- **Intraday or rapid trading** — a fund that bought and sold the same stock five times during the quarter just shows up as whatever it held at the final snapshot.

In short: a 13F is a single, low-resolution, time-delayed photograph of part of a portfolio. Treating it as a live signal is like reading a 45-day-old newspaper and reacting as if it's breaking news.

id: smart-money/02-13f-filings#So why does "cloning" come up at all?
title: What a 13F filing tells you
heading: So why does "cloning" come up at all?
text:
Here's where it gets genuinely interesting. There's real academic research looking at what happens if you mechanically copy the *changes* in holdings of certain types of investors — specifically, ones with **low turnover** (they don't trade often) and **concentrated** portfolios (a small number of large positions, suggesting high conviction). Some studies have found that portfolios constructed this way — buying what such investors bought, after the 13F becomes public, and holding for a similar long period — have historically shown excess returns compared to a simple market benchmark, *on average, over long periods*.

Two things are doing a lot of work in that sentence: "low turnover, concentrated" and "on average, over long periods." This research is about a *style* — patient, high-conviction, long-term investors — not about copying *any* 13F, and not about any single trade.

title: What a 13F filing tells you
heading: Higher-signal cousins: 13Ds and insider cluster-buys
text:
Two related types of filings are generally considered more timely and information-rich than ordinary 13Fs:

- **Form 13D** is filed when an investor acquires more than 5% of a company *and* intends to influence or control it (an "activist" stake) — filed within days, not 45 days later, and signals active intent, not just passive holding.
- **Insider cluster buying** — when *multiple* executives or directors of a company buy shares with their own money around the same time — is often considered more informative than a single insider's trade, since it's harder to explain away as personal financial planning when several people make the same call independently.

Both are examples of filings that are faster and carry more "intent" information than a routine 13F — which is part of why treating all filings as equally meaningful is a mistake.

title: What a 13F filing tells you
heading: The takeaway
text: 13F season makes for great headlines because "what the smart money bought" is an irresistible story. The reality is more like: a quarterly, partial, 45-day-old photo of part of a portfolio, most useful when looking at *patterns over time* from *patient, concentrated* investors — and even then, as one input into your own thinking, never a signal to act on by itself.
